does: This paper introduces Support Vector Rubrics (SVR), a framework that generates evaluation rubrics for assessing large language model outputs by learning to discriminate between similar responses rather than simply describing good ones. The core innovation recasts rubric construction as a max-margin boundary learning problem over preference pairs, mining contrastive features into a rubric bank while learning a prompt-conditioned selector and global weights. The method iteratively refines the rubric bank through support-pair selection and adversarial mining of hard negatives. At inference, SVR retrieves relevant rubrics for a given prompt and uses them to score responses. Experiments on RubricBench demonstrate substantial improvements, reducing the gap between self-generated and human reference rubrics from 24.1 to 0.3 points and outperforming self-rubric and judge baselines, while the learned rubric bank transfers across different judges without retraining.
connects: This work builds on the emerging paradigm of rubric-based evaluation for LLMs, which has become an alternative to traditional judge models for assessing output quality. It extends prior work on self-generated evaluation criteria by diagnosing a fundamental objective mismatch: existing methods optimize for describing desirable properties rather than discriminating between candidates with similar quality. The paper leverages machine learning techniques from support vector machines and preference learning, drawing on insights from contrastive learning and active learning for hard example selection. It also builds on recent work in learning-to-rank and reward modeling that use preference pairs to train evaluators, adapting these techniques to the rubric generation setting where interpretability and modularity of criteria are valued.
enables: This work opens several directions for future research in LLM evaluation. The discriminative rubric framework could extend to multi-aspect evaluation where different rubrics target specific dimensions of quality, enabling more nuanced feedback generation. The adversarial hard-negative mining approach might inspire methods for identifying failure modes in evaluation systems themselves. The transferability of learned rubric banks across judges suggests potential for building general-purpose evaluation libraries that can be adapted to domain-specific needs with minimal retraining. Additionally, the explicit representation of evaluation criteria as modular rubrics could enable better interpretability and debugging of model outputs, as well as provide richer feedback signals for fine-tuning language models beyond simple scalar rewards.
